raw_case:
Spontaneous Pneumomediastinum in a Patient With Undiagnosed Ankylosing Spondylitis
Spontaneous pneumomediastinum (SPM) is an uncommon condition characterized by air in the interstices of the mediastinum. Management generally involves supportive care; however, if a patient inspires high concentrations of oxygen, the mediastinal air will be absorbed faster. A 23-year-old man who presented with acute-onset breathlessness with a history of more than a year of lower backache was diagnosed with SPM and accompanying ankylosing spondylitis (AS) by a chest CT and spinal MRI and was treated conservatively. This case is being reported for its uniqueness, as SPM with underlying AS is rare.

Introduction

Pneumomediastinum is defined as the presence of air in the mediastinum. It is classified into two categories: spontaneous pneumomediastinum (SPM) and secondary pneumomediastinum [1]. The term “spontaneous pneumomediastinum” is used when there is no obvious antecedent cause (such as surgery, infection, trauma, or viscus perforation) in otherwise healthy individuals. The condition is often misdiagnosed due to the vague nature of its presentation. SPM must be included in the differential diagnosis of chest pain to enable prompt management [2]. Chest discomfort and dyspnea, two of the most common presenting symptoms of SPM, can be caused by a variety of cardiopulmonary illnesses, making the diagnosis of SPM challenging. Despite the rarity of SPM, early identification and treatment are critical to prevent worsening of the condition.

Case presentation

A 23-year-old man with a history of lower backache of more than one year presented to our emergency department with acute-onset breathlessness beginning one day before. His blood pressure was 120/80 mmHg, temperature was 97.9ºF, respiratory rate was 30 cycles/min, pulse rate was 134 BPM, and oxygen saturation was 88% on room air. The patient was tachypneic, and subcutaneous crepitus was felt on examination. On auscultation, crackles were heard in the bilateral interscapular and mammary regions, accompanied by a pericardial rub. His laboratory values were remarkable, with a total leukocyte count of 14,000/dL. A chest X-ray revealed pneumomediastinum (Figure 1), and a chest CT was remarkable for bilateral subcutaneous emphysema and moderate pneumomediastinum (with pneumopericardium), with air tracking along the bilateral lung fields (predominantly along bilateral oblique fissures) and patchy areas of ground-glass opacities in the left upper lobe and anteromedial segment of the left lower lobe (Figure 2).An echocardiogram was performed, which was normal. The patient was managed with IV antibiotics for five days, along with oxygen support at 10 L/min for the first two days, after which his oxygen requirement decreased and his symptoms improved. In light of the patient’s history of more than one year of persistent lower backache with morning stiffness, a pelvic X-ray (Figure 3) and spinal MRI (Figure 4) were conducted, which revealed bilateral sacroiliitis. In light of the MRI findings, a test for HLA-B27 was ordered, which returned positive. The patient is currently being managed with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for lower backache. A repeat chest CT was performed, which showed a resolution of the patient’s pneumomediastinum (Figures 5, 6). 

Discussion

Pneumomediastinum (also known as mediastinal emphysema) is characterized by the presence of air in the mediastinum [2]. SPM is a rare condition with a male-to-female (M:F) ratio of 8:13 and is typically linked to underlying respiratory conditions such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and bronchial asthma [3]. SPM usually presents with chest discomfort and dyspnea. Other symptoms include cough, dysphagia, a nasal voice, dysphonia, and cervical pain [4]. In this case, dyspnea was the only presenting symptom. In terms of diagnostic investigation, a chest CT picks up pneumomediastinum in most cases if an ordered chest X-ray is inconclusive. In the present case, a chest X-ray showed features of pneumomediastinum that were confirmed by chest CT. Along with features of pneumomediastinum, patchy areas of ground glass opacities were observed in a few segments of the left lung. The patient did not have any underlying respiratory symptoms prior to this acute event. Serious illnesses, such as pulmonary embolism, acute coronary syndrome, primary pneumothorax, and pericarditis, are included in the differential diagnosis of SPM. Upper lobe fibrosis, spontaneous pneumothorax, sleep apnea, ventilatory dysfunction due to chest wall restriction, and interstitial lung disease are other pulmonary manifestations of the disease [5]. With the enhanced lung parenchyma visibility made possible by high-resolution CT, interstitial lung disease is now a recognized feature of lung involvement in AS, in addition to apical fibrosis. These conditions were ruled out in our patient with a chest CT and 2D echocardiography. The patient responded to supportive management with oxygen therapy, which eventually led to a complete resolution of symptoms, as is observed in most cases. The patient was further evaluated for inflammatory back pain with a spinal MRI, which revealed features of sacroiliitis. A subsequent HLA B-27 test was found to be positive. The patient was thus diagnosed with ankylosing spondylitis (AS).

Conclusions

In this case report, we present a rare instance of SPM in a patient with AS. The development of pneumomediastinum in AS patients is unusual and warrants attention due to the potential complications. The patient’s presentation with chest pain and dyspnea led to the diagnosis, confirmed by imaging studies, which revealed air in the mediastinum without any apparent traumatic or iatrogenic cause. Conservative management was successful, with the patient recovering without any surgical intervention.This case highlights the importance of considering pneumomediastinum in the differential diagnosis of chest pain in AS patients. Early recognition and appropriate management are crucial to prevent complications. Further studies are needed to understand the association between AS and SPM and to develop guidelines for its management in this patient population.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 23-year-old male
- Chief Complaint: Acute-onset breathlessness
- History of Present Illness: Acute breathlessness starting one day prior to presentation; history of lower backache with morning stiffness lasting more than one year.
- Past Medical History: None reported
- Personal History: None mentioned
- Family History: None mentioned
- Physical Examination: Tachypnea (respiratory rate 30 cycles/min), oxygen saturation 88% on room air, subcutaneous crepitus on palpation, bilateral crackles on auscultation, pericardial rub.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Chest X-ray and CT: Confirmed moderate pneumomediastinum, bilateral subcutaneous emphysema, pneumopericardium, air tracking in lung fields, and ground-glass opacities in specific lung areas.
  - Echocardiogram: Normal findings.
  - Pelvic X-ray and spinal MRI: Bilateral sacroiliitis.
  - HLA-B27 test: Positive.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Pneumomediastinum (SPM):** Confirmed by chest imaging showing air in the mediastinum without obvious traumatic or infectious causes and with typical symptoms such as dyspnea and chest discomfort. Ruled out secondary pneumomediastinum due to lack of evident antecedent causes such as trauma or infection. 
   
2. **Pulmonary embolism/acute coronary syndrome/pericarditis:** Excluded by imaging (chest CT, 2D echocardiogram) and lack of clinical signs specific to these conditions (e.g., normal findings on echocardiogram for cardiac causes).

3. **Primary pneumothorax:** Differentiated from SPM via chest CT findings showing no evidence of pneumothorax but confirmation of pneumomediastinum.

4. **Underlying respiratory conditions (e.g., asthma, COPD):** No prior history, symptoms, or imaging findings supporting these conditions.

5. **Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS):** Considered due to chronic lower backache with morning stiffness. Confirmed by spinal MRI findings of sacroiliitis and positive HLA-B27 test.
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
- **Spontaneous Pneumomediastinum (SPM):** Based on characteristic imaging findings, absence of trauma or infections, and successful response to supportive care (oxygen therapy).
- **Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS):** Diagnosed due to chronic inflammatory back pain with sacroiliitis on MRI and positive HLA-B27 status.

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's try to work through this case step by step. The patient is a 23-year-old male with acute breathlessness that started a day before presentation. He also has a history of lower backache with morning stiffness for over a year. His physical exam shows tachypnea, low oxygen saturation, subcutaneous crepitus, bilateral crackles, and a pericardial rub. The tests show pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema, pneumopericardium, air in the lungs, ground-glass opacities, normal echocardiogram, bilateral sacroiliitis on imaging, and positive HLA-B27.

First, the acute breathlessness with subcutaneous crepitus and pneumomediastinum makes me think of something causing air to leak into the mediastinum. Common causes include trauma, infection, or maybe a spontaneous pneumothorax. But the presence of subcutaneous emphysema and pneumopericardium suggests that the air is spreading beyond the lungs, possibly from a ruptured alveolus or a bronchus. However, the patient doesn't mention trauma, so maybe something like a spontaneous pneumothorax with a large leak? But why would there be sacroiliitis and HLA-B27 positive?

Wait, the patient also has a long history of lower back pain and morning stiffness. Sacroiliitis is a key finding in conditions like ankylosing spondylitis, which is associated with HLA-B27. So that points towards a rheumatologic condition. But how does that connect with the acute respiratory symptoms?

Ankylosing spondylitis can lead to complications like pulmonary involvement, but typically it's more about restrictive lung disease or fibrosis. However, I'm not sure about a direct link to pneumomediastinum. Alternatively, maybe there's an infection or an autoimmune process causing lung damage. But the HLA-B27 is more specific for spondyloarthropathies.

Another angle: the presence of air in the mediastinum and pericardium could be due to a ruptured esophagus or a lung abscess. But the patient doesn't have a history of trauma or known infection. The ground-glass opacities might suggest infection or interstitial lung disease. However, the combination with sacroiliitis and HLA-B27 is puzzling.

Wait, could there be a rare association between ankylosing spondylitis and spontaneous pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum? I'm not sure. Alternatively, maybe the patient has a connective tissue disorder that predisposes to air leaks. But the HLA-B27 is more specific for the sacroiliitis.

Alternatively, could the acute breathlessness be due to a complication of ankylosing spondylitis? For example, if there's a severe case leading to respiratory compromise, but the immediate cause here is the pneumomediastinum and subcutaneous emphysema. Maybe the patient had a recent exacerbation of his back condition that led to some respiratory muscle involvement? But that doesn't directly explain the air leak.

Wait, another possibility: the patient might have a condition called Pneumothorax associated with connective tissue diseases. But again, the HLA-B27 is more about the sacroiliitis. Alternatively, maybe the patient has a rare condition like Behçet's disease, which can cause both sacroiliitis and pulmonary involvement, but Behçet's is usually associated with other symptoms like oral ulcers or genital ulcers, which aren't mentioned here.

Alternatively, could the sacroiliitis be a red herring, and the main issue is a primary pulmonary problem? But the presence of HLA-B27 and sacroiliitis is significant. Maybe the patient has ankylosing spondylitis with a concurrent spontaneous pneumothorax. But the presence of pneumopericardium and subcutaneous emphysema suggests that the air is escaping from the lungs into the mediastinum and pericardium, which is a severe case of pneumothorax or maybe a bronchopleural fistula.

Wait, but the chest X-ray and CT show ground-glass opacities. That might indicate some kind of infection or interstitial process. However, the presence of air in the mediastinum and pericardium is more concerning for a rupture. Maybe the patient had a recent viral infection that caused interstitial lung disease, leading to alveolar rupture and subsequent air leak. But the HLA-B27 is still a puzzle here.

Putting it all together: The acute presentation with pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema, and pneumopericardium is a severe air leak syndrome. The underlying chronic condition is ankylosing spondylitis (due to sacroiliitis and HLA-B27). Maybe the patient's chronic disease led to some structural changes in the lungs, making them more susceptible to air leaks. Alternatively, the acute presentation is a separate event, and the sacroiliitis is coincidental. However, the presence of HLA-B27 and sacroiliitis is a strong indicator of ankylosing spondylitis, which is a systemic condition. So the final diagnosis would be ankylosing spondylitis with acute spontaneous pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum. But the presence of pneumopericardium and subcutaneous emphysema might point to a more extensive air leak, possibly from a ruptured alveolus due to some underlying lung pathology.

Alternatively, could the patient have a condition like Wegener's granulomatosis, which can cause both pulmonary involvement and sacroiliitis? But that's less likely without other symptoms like sinus issues or kidney involvement.

Wait, the HLA-B27 is positive and sacroiliitis is present, so ankylosing spondylitis is the most likely diagnosis. The acute symptoms are due to a separate issue, but maybe the patient's lung involvement is part of the spondyloarthropathy. However, I'm not sure about the direct link between ankylosing spondylitis and pneumomediastinum. Alternatively, the patient might have a different condition that's causing both the sacroiliitis and the air leak. But I can't think of a direct connection. Maybe the answer is that the patient has ankylosing spondylitis (due to sacroiliitis and HLA-B27) and acute spontaneous pneumomediastinum with subcutaneous emphysema and pneumopericardium, which is a separate event. But the question asks for the final diagnosis, which might be combining both the chronic and acute findings. However, the main acute issue is the air leak syndrome. So the final diagnosis would be ankylosing spondylitis with acute spontaneous pneumomediastinum and subcutaneous emphysema, and pneumopericardium. But I need to make sure that the air leak is not due to something else. Alternatively, the answer might be that the patient has ankylosing spondylitis (based on sacroiliitis and HLA-B27) and the acute presentation is a spontaneous pneumothorax with air tracking, but the presence of pneumopericardium and subcutaneous emphysema suggests a more severe air leak. However, the exact diagnosis for the acute part is pneumomediastinum with subcutaneous emphysema and pneumopericardium, which could be due to a ruptured alveolus or bronchus. The chronic condition is ankylosing spondylitis. Therefore, the final diagnosis is ankylosing spondylitis with acute spontaneous pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema, and pneumopericardium.

### Resoning:
<step 1> The acute presentation of breathlessness, tachypnea, hypoxia, subcutaneous crepitus, and findings of pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema, and pneumopericardium on imaging strongly suggest a spontaneous air leak syndrome, most likely due to a ruptured alveolus or bronchus. The presence of bilateral crackles and ground-glass opacities may indicate concurrent interstitial lung involvement or infection, though no infectious etiology is explicitly mentioned.  
<step 2> The chronic history of lower backache with morning stiffness and bilateral sacroiliitis on pelvic X-ray and spinal MRI, along with a positive HLA-B27 test, points to ankylosing spondylitis (AS), a seronegative spondyloarthropathy. AS is a systemic condition that can involve the axial skeleton and, less commonly, the lungs. However, the acute air leak syndrome is not a typical feature of AS.  
<step 3> The combination of AS and the acute air leak syndrome raises the possibility of an underlying connective tissue disorder or autoimmune condition that predisposes to both pulmonary and musculoskeletal manifestations. However, the acute symptoms are temporally distinct from the chronic musculoskeletal disease.  
<step 4> While the air leak syndrome is likely idiopathic or secondary to an undetermined cause (e.g., viral infection, trauma, or interstitial lung disease), the presence of HLA-B27 and sacroiliitis confirms AS as the chronic underlying condition. The acute findings are most consistent with spontaneous pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum, which may occur in the setting of interstitial lung disease or alveolar rupture, though no direct link to AS is established.
